one_line_takeaway: Prompt-injection guard models over-trigger on benign sentences that merely contain attack-associated words; the paper quantifies this with the 339-sample NotInject benchmark and fixes it with InjecGuard's MOF training, reaching 83.48% average accuracy (over-defense 87.32%) and beating the prior open-source SotA by 30.83%.
key_finding: Existing prompt guard models suffer severe over-defense: over-defense accuracy is 5.60% (Fmops), 5.31% (Deepset), 0.88% (PromptGuard), and 56.64% (ProtectAIv2, the prior open-source SotA) -- at or below the 50% random-guessing level for the open-source guards. InjecGuard reaches 87.32% over-defense, 85.74% benign, and 77.39% malicious accuracy for an 83.48% average, beating open-source runner-up ProtectAIv2 by 30.83% on average (and by 54.17% on over-defense) and the commercial LakeraGuard by 6.25% on average, while roughly matching GPT-4o's 85.53% average at ~503x better efficiency (15.34 ms vs 7907.18 ms inference). Ablations show data-centric augmentation alone actually HURTS over-defense (75.22% -> 64.31%), but adding MOF with retrain-from-scratch lifts the average to the final 83.48%; MOF also beats a conventional shortcut-mitigation baseline, which raised over-defense to 86.73% but cut malicious accuracy to 65.53%, whereas MOF holds malicious at 77.39%.
core_contribution: The paper makes two coupled contributions to the prompt-injection-defense literature. (1) NotInject, an evaluation dataset of 339 benign sentences deliberately seeded with words that frequently appear in prompt-injection attacks (e.g., 'ignore', 'cancel'), built specifically to measure 'over-defense' -- the tendency of prompt guard models to flag such benign inputs as malicious. It is split into three difficulty tiers of 113 samples each, containing 1, 2, and 3 trigger words respectively. (2) InjecGuard, an open-source DeBERTaV3-base prompt guard model trained with a new strategy called Mitigating Over-defense for Free (MOF) plus a data-centric augmentation step, which the authors claim is the first prompt guard model to release its full training dataset, strategy, code, and weights. These are the paper's OWN artifacts, distinct from the cited prior guards (ProtectAIv2, PromptGuard, Deepset, Fmops, LakeraGuard) which it evaluates as baselines.
threat_model: The paper sits inside the prompt-injection defense setting: an attacker inserts malicious instructions into an LLM's natural-language input to cause goal hijacking or data leakage, and prompt guard models are lightweight text classifiers deployed upstream to reject such inputs before they reach the victim LLM. Crucially, the paper's own focus is NOT a new attack but a failure mode of the defense itself: 'over-defense,' defined by the authors as the tendency of a guard model to predict a malicious label when a benign sentence merely contains trigger words common in injection attacks. This is a false-positive / usability problem (rejecting legitimate users, e.g., in virtual assistants or medical tools), which the authors trace to shortcut learning -- the guard assigning excessive attention to individual trigger tokens rather than sentence meaning.
limitations: The authors' own stated limitation is that NotInject, despite careful design, may not capture the full diversity of real-world benign inputs, especially in domain-specific settings, so it could under-estimate over-defense in sensitive fields like healthcare or finance; they plan to add domain-specific data via industry collaboration. Beyond their statement: the entire NotInject and MOF pipeline leans heavily on GPT-4o-mini both to generate benign test/training sentences and to filter them, so the benchmark and the model's mitigation data share a generator, and 'benign' labels rest partly on LLM self-assessment plus manual review. Trigger-word identification depends on the chosen Dm/Db corpora, and malicious accuracy (77.39%) remains the weakest of InjecGuard's three axes and is lower than several baselines on malicious detection alone (e.g., PromptGuard 97.10%, Fmops 93.50%), reflecting an over-defense / detection trade-off the paper improves but does not eliminate.

datasets_used: NotInject (introduced here; 339 benign samples, 113 each for 1/2/3 trigger-word subsets, from 113 refined trigger words)
PINT benchmark (LakeraAI) -- benign and malicious eval
WildGuard benchmark (Han et al., 2024) -- benign eval

Training set: 20 open-source datasets incl. Alpaca, Safeguard-Injection (Erdogan et al.), TaskTrack (Abdelnabi et al.); final 61,089 benign + 15,666 injection samples, of which 435 from data-centric augmentation across 17 input formats
quantitative_results: Beats open-source runner-up ProtectAIv2 by 30.83% average and 54.17% on over-defense; beats commercial LakeraGuard by 6.25% average
Baseline over-defense accuracy: Fmops 5.60%, Deepset 5.31%, PromptGuard 0.88%, ProtectAIv2 56.64%, LakeraGuard 87.61%, LlamaGuard3 99.71%, GPT-4o 86.73%, Llama-2-chat 76.40%
Baseline average accuracy: Fmops 44.58%, Deepset 43.62%, PromptGuard 41.60%, ProtectAIv2 63.81%, LakeraGuard 77.23%, GPT-4o 85.53%, LlamaGuard3 74.39%, Llama-2-chat 56.17%
Efficiency: InjecGuard 15.34 ms inference, 60.45 GFLOPs, efficiency score 5.44 (~503x GPT-4o's 0.01; GPT-4o 7907.18 ms)

Ablation (Table 2): basic training avg 74.64% (over-def 75.22/benign 78.53/mal 70.17); +data-centric aug avg 73.87% (over-def drops to 64.31); +MOF scratch retrain avg 83.48% (87.32/85.74/77.39); MOF-scratch on basic-only avg 81.89% (89.38/84.73/71.57)
Shortcut-mitigation comparison (Table 3): w/o MOF over-def 64.31 / mal 75.95; +conventional shortcut mitigation 86.73 / 65.53; +MOF 87.32 / 77.39
baselines_compared: Conventional shortcut-mitigation method (Wang et al., 2021) applied to InjecGuard
